title: Competition and Price Variation when Consumers Are Loss Averse
abstract: We modify the Salop (1979) model of price competition with differentiated products by assuming that consumers are loss averse relative to a reference point given by their recent expectations about the purchase. Consumers' sensitivity to losses in money increases the price responsiveness of demand—and hence the intensity of competition—at higher relative to lower market prices, reducing or eliminating price variation both within and between products. When firms face common stochastic costs, in any symmetric equilibrium the markup is strictly decreasing in cost. Even when firms face different cost distributions, we identify conditions under which a focal-price equilibrium (where firms always charge the same “focal” price) exists, and conditions under which any equilibrium is focal. (JEL D11, D43, D81, L13)
summary_en: Why do many firms charge roughly the same price most of the time, with occasional sales? This paper modifies the classic price-competition model to allow for loss-averse consumers who compare actual prices against their recently-formed expectations. Consumer loss aversion makes demand more responsive to higher prices — intensifying competition at the top of the price distribution. The result: prices cluster at a "focal" price, with very little within-product or across-product variation, even when firms face different costs. A behavioural foundation for the observed price stickiness in many consumer-goods markets.
data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Predictions match observed price-setting in consumer-goods retail.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian competition and price-regulation analysis: the paper suggests that the observed lack of price dispersion in some retail markets (FMCG, petrol) is not necessarily evidence of collusion; loss aversion provides a competitive explanation. Relevant to GVH assessments of parallel pricing. Caveat: the model doesn't rule out genuine collusion — it just raises the evidentiary bar for inferring anti-competitive behaviour from pure price parallelism. Careful sectoral analysis still needed.
